data.title: Senate Democrat: 'Noem's got to go'
data.description:
Sen. Peter Welch (D-Vt.) called for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to be removed as the leader of the government agency that oversees immigration enforcement operations, saying he would support her impeachment if she doesn’t resign.

In an interview Monday on MS NOW’s “Ana Cabrera Reports,” the Democratic senator called Noem “the real problem” as he criticized her handling of the fatal federal officer-involved shootings this month of Renee Good and Alex Pretti, both 37-year-old Minneapolis residents who died while protesting immigration operations in their city.

“Noem’s got to go. I mean, we have the head of this department who spends a lot of her time in self-promotion, the $200 million video shoot in Mount Rushmore,” Welch said in the interview. “And then when there is a serious, serious situation involving the death of two [Minnesota] citizens, she calls them domestic terrorists.”

“No one has confidence that she’s doing this job on the level,” the senator added.

Host Ana Cabrera noted that Noem was unlikely to resign and asked whether Welch would support impeaching the secretary.

“I would, if that’s what it took,” Welch said, “but she should resign.”

Welch said Noem has “blown the job” of Homeland Security secretary, characterizing the immigration crackdown as “mass intimidation,” not effective law enforcement.  

“She is the real problem because what she’s doing is turning the function of immigration enforcement away from enforcement and into being kind of a shock troop that goes into cities and acts with impunity,” Welch said. “She’s blown the job. You know, we need immigration enforcement, but that’s not what she’s doing. It’s mass intimidation.”

Welch is the latest Democrat to respond to the fatal shootings in Minneapolis by calling for Noem’s impeachment.

The Democratic Party’s official account on the social platform X backed the push for Noem’s impeachment, writing in a post Sunday, “IMPEACH KRISTI NOEM.”

The Hill has reached out to DHS for a response to Welch’s comments.

In response to other calls for Noem’s impeachment, Homeland Security Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin said, “DHS enforces the laws Congress passes, period.”

“If certain members don’t like those laws, changing them is literally their job,” she added, in the statement.

data.title: Senate Democrat: 'Real domestic terrorists' are in the White House
data.description:
Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-Md.) on Sunday slammed the White House response to the killing of Alex Pretti, an intensive care nurse who was protesting the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown in Minneapolis.

Senior Trump officials including Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen Miller and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem quickly labelled Pretti a “domestic terrorist,” echoing the language they used to describe Renee Good, who was killed by an immigration officer earlier this month.

“This has become part of the disgusting White House cover-up of these killings. They said the same thing about Renee Good. They really think the American people are stupid. I mean, we can all see the videotapes,” Van Hollen said during an appearance on MS Now’s “The Weekend.”

“We saw the videotapes with Renee Good. We’ve seen the videotape today with Alex Pretti. It is clear that Stephen Miller and this White House are engaged in a cover-up, and it’s criminal to try to cover up a killing like this. Look, the reality is that the real domestic terrorists, they’re in the White House right now,” he added.

Good, a mother of three, was shot while trying to drive away from ICE officers. Trump officials accused her of trying to use her vehicle as a weapon.

Pretti, who was legally carrying a handgun in his waistband, was shot at point-blank range while trying to protect a woman being pursued by immigration agents. He had been recording immigration enforcement operations in Minneapolis, Minn.

Van Hollen has been a leading critic of the Trump administration’s immigration agenda, visiting Maryland resident Kilmar Abrego Garcia, who was deported to El Salvador’s maximum security CECOT prison last year. Abrego Garcia has since been returned home to the U.S.

Van Hollen on Sunday railed against Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations, calling the recent footage of incidents in Minnesota as “sickening.”

“We need to send a message loud and clear that accountability will eventually catch up to people, because what’s happening right now is the Trump administration has essentially said to folks at ICE, anything goes,” he said.  

“You can do anything. You have a license to kill. And by the way, we won’t investigate you seriously. We won’t allow independent investigations. And, you know, we’ll take care of this for you. That’s what’s happening,” he added.

data.title: Paul calls on leaders of ICE, Border Protection and Immigration Services to testify before Senate
data.description:
Senate Homeland Security Committee Chair Rand Paul (R-Ky.) has sent letters to the heads of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), Customs and Border Protection (CBP) and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) to testify before his committee amid growing public controversy over immigration enforcement operations in Minneapolis and other communities.

Paul informed the leaders of President Trump’s immigration agencies in a tersely worded letter that “Congress has an obligation to conduct oversight” of what he called the “exceptional amount of funding” the Republican Congress has allocated to secure the borders and enforce immigration laws.

“Congress has an obligation to conduct oversight of those tax dollars and ensure the funding is used to accomplish the mission, provide proper support for our law enforcement, and, most importantly, protect the American people,” he wrote.

He is asking them to testify at an open hearing scheduled for Feb. 12 and wants to get responses from the senior administration officials by Wednesday.

Paul sent the letters to CBP Commissioner Rodney Scott, ICE acting Director Todd Lyons, and USCIS Director Joseph Edlow.

The conservative Kentucky senator marveled at the decision by congressional appropriators to spend another $10 billion on ICE operations in fiscal 2026, even though the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which Trump signed into law in July, has already allocated $76 billion for ICE over the next four years.

“The Senate is debating another $10B for ICE. But Congress already locked in nearly $19B a year for four years. Even if this fails, ICE will still have about 87% more funding than last year,” Paul posted Monday on the social platform X.

Paul, an outspoken advocate for the Fourth Amendment, which guarantees a person’s right against unreasonable searches and seizures, has kept a relatively low profile on ICE’s immigration enforcement operations in Minneapolis, despite a recent ruling by a federal judge in Minnesota that officers violated a Liberian man’s rights by forcibly entering his home without a judicial warrant.

The man who was arrested, Garrison Gibson, had fled Liberia as a child and remained in the country under an order of supervision.

The fatal shooting of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, on Saturday has put heightened scrutiny on ICE’s and the Border Patrol’s immigration enforcement operations in Minneapolis.

Several Senate Republicans have demanded a thorough and objective investigation of the shooting incident, while one senior House Republican lawmaker, Rep. James Comer (Ky.), suggested that Trump should consider pulling ICE personnel out of Minnesota.

Comer, the chair of the House Oversight Committee, said in an interview with Maria Bartiromo on Fox News’s “Sunday Morning Futures” that other cities that “have issues with criminal illegals” would be more receptive to immigration enforcement operations.

data.title: Beshear: 'Retrain every single' federal immigration officer
data.description:
Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear (D) pushed for the retraining of every federal immigration officer in the wake of two shooting deaths this month at the hands of federal immigration authorities in Minneapolis.

“Well, the first thing we have to do is demand that they pull these officers out of all of our cities and that they fully retrain every single one of them. They are operating with aggressive tactics that are not appropriate for law enforcement. They are not following our Constitution in giving people their rights,” Beshear said Monday on “CNN News Central.” 

“The idea that they have a memo that says they can just barge into someone’s house without a real warrant is — I mean, I guess they missed the day they taught law in law school. It’s incredibly concerning. And so, what they need to do is a full-on retraining.”

Beshear’s comments follow the deadly shooting of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse who lived in Minneapolis, by a Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agent over the weekend. 

Pretti’s death came just a few weeks after the fatal shooting of a woman named Renee Good by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer in the same city. Good was also 37 years old.

Good and Pretti’s deaths have caused widespread outrage directed at President Trump’s strict immigration agenda, which had already received heavy criticism before the shootings.

Trump on Monday said he had a “very good call” with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D), adding they appeared “to be on a similar wavelength” amid high tensions in the state.

“Governor Tim Walz called me with the request to work together with respect to Minnesota. It was a very good call, and we, actually, seemed to be on a similar wavelength,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

The Hill has reached out to the Department of Homeland Security for comment.

data.title: Fetterman slams Minneapolis immigration operation but breaks from Democrats on shutdown threat
data.description:
Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.), a frequent independent voice among Senate Democrats, condemned the federal immigration crackdown in Minnesota but said he is unwilling to force a government shutdown over funding disputes involving the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

In a statement Monday, the Pennsylvania Democrat called for the federal operation to end, as he mourned the lives of Renee Good and Alex Pretti, both 37-year-old Minneapolis residents who died in shootings by federal immigration officers. They were both U.S. citizens who participated in demonstrations against Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations in the city.

“Ms. Good and Mr. Pretti should still be alive. My family grieves for theirs,” Fetterman said in a statement. “The operation in Minneapolis should stand down and immediately end. It has become an ungovernable and dangerous urban theatre for civilians and law enforcement that is incompatible with the American spirit.”

Lawmakers had been on the verge of completing their funding work for fiscal 2026 this week after the House passed its final appropriations bills. But the Pretti shooting on Saturday appears to have jeopardized chances of those bills passing the Senate, with one key Democrat after another saying they will not vote to fund DHS.

The DHS bill is tied in with five others that fund the departments of Defense, Transportation, Housing and Urban Development, Health and Human Services, Labor and Education — all of which have broad support.

But Senate Democrats are now proposing separating the DHS funding bill from the larger appropriations package, saying they won’t vote for a so-called “minibus” that includes funding for DHS. Congress has until Friday to pass the remaining six funding bills and avert a partial government shutdown.

Fetterman said he supports separating the DHS bill from the larger package but would not join his colleagues in voting against the current version of the appropriations package, in the likely event that Republicans don’t agree to separate DHS funding.

In his statement, Fetterman said he’s “spent significant time hearing many different positions on the funding bills and maintain that I will never vote to shut our government down, especially our Defense Department.”

“I reject the calls to defund or abolish ICE. I strongly disagree with many strategies and practices ICE deployed in Minneapolis, and believe that must change,” he added, noting “a vote to shut our government down will not defund ICE” because Trump’s “One Big Beautiful Bill Act” provided billions of dollars in funding for DHS.

“I want a conversation on the DHS appropriations bill and support stripping it from the minibus,” Fetterman said, but, “It is unlikely that will happen and our country will suffer another shutdown.”

“We must find a way forward and I remain committed to being a voice of reason and common sense,” he added.

data.title: Trump faces growing GOP backlash over immigration crackdown after second deadly shooting
data.description:
The Republican Party’s determined defense of President Trump’s deportation efforts is cracking in the wake of the fatal shooting of a second civilian protester in Minneapolis over the weekend.

For the past year, GOP lawmakers on Capitol Hill have been virtually united in supporting Trump’s immigrant crackdown, saying the deployment of federal law enforcement officers in blue cities around the country was a necessary step for protecting Americans against violent criminals living in the U.S. illegally. Last summer, they provided tens of billions of dollars to help the cause. 

But the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse and U.S. citizen, by a Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agent on Saturday has sparked outrage that’s transcending party lines, leading even some Republicans to voice concerns that Trump’s deportation push has gone too far. 

“People want stability and they want confidence — and that does not look like stability or confidence,” a source familiar with the administration said, adding that they believe it is “inevitable” that the administration cooperates with a probe into the shooting.

“Half my Instagram stories are people … who are posting ‘F ICE,’” the source continued. “It’s clearly cracked the traditional media sphere. I think the White House and the administration recognizes that.”

Underscoring that development is a long and growing list of Republicans who typically side squarely with the administration but are now urging officials to engage in a transparent investigation and to take additional steps to prevent future tragedies. 

In the House, Rep. Mike McCaul (R-Texas), the former head of the Homeland Security Committee, said he is “troubled” by Pretti’s killing. Oversight Committee Chair James Comer (R-Ky.) went so far as to suggest that Trump should pull U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) personnel from the Twin Cities amid the ongoing strife. And Homeland Security Committee Chair Andrew Garbarino (R-N.Y.) quickly called on officials from ICE, CBP and the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) to testify before the committee after the shooting on Saturday. 

“Congress has an important responsibility to ensure the safety of law enforcement and the people they serve and protect,” Garbarino wrote in a statement.

The GOP cracks are also emerging on the other side of the Capitol, where the shooting has prompted blowback from a growing list of conservative Republicans, not just the centrist senators — Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska), Susan Collins (R-Maine) and Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) — who are frequent Trump critics. 

Indeed, Sens. David McCormick (R-Pa.) and Pete Ricketts (R-Neb.) — a pair of lawmakers who keep a low profile on Capitol Hill — both said that an investigation was needed, though they emphasized their continued support for Homeland Security funding in the meantime.

Freshman GOP Sen. John Curtis (R-Utah) on Monday dinged Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem for her “premature” response to the shooting, which he said “weakened confidence.”

And Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.), the chairman of the Senate Homeland Security Committee, followed Garbarino’s example and called the heads of ICE, Border Patrol and USCIS to testify before his panel on Feb. 12.

McCormick also aligned himself with the National Rifle Association, which has pushed back forcefully against the administration’s argument that Pretti posed a deadly threat to the federal officers because he was carrying a gun. Bearing arms is protected by the Second Amendment, the NRA said, and Pretti had a valid concealed carry permit. 

Bill Essayli, First Assistant U.S. Attorney for the Central District of California, wrote on X that, “If you approach law enforcement with a gun, there is a high likelihood they will be legally justified in shooting you.”

But several Republicans pushed back against Essayli, arguing that simply carrying a gun does not constitute grounds for an official to use deadly force. 

“Carrying a firearm is not a death sentence, it’s a Constitutionally protected God-given right, and if you don’t understand this you have no business in law enforcement or government,” Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) wrote on X.

Rep. Chip Roy (R-Texas) echoed that message, telling Fox News over the weekend, “Look, I am a Second Amendment supporter and I want to say I don’t agree with that sentiment exactly. You can be armed. You can be in public. We are Second Amendment supporters, so I disagree with that.”

Pretti’s killing came as hostilities between the Trump administration and Democratic lawmakers — federal, state and local — are burning white hot over the president’s aggressive deportation surge, which has targeted blue cities around the country. Minneapolis has been a particular hotbed of those tensions, after an ICE officer fatally shot an unarmed 37-year-old woman earlier in the month. The administration alleges Renee Good disobeyed ICE officers’ commands and used her car as a deadly weapon; videos appear to show her turning her vehicle away from officers before shots were fired.

The back-to-back killings of U.S. citizens in the name of tackling illegal immigration has horrified much of the country; amplified Democratic calls to impeach Noem; and given new fuel to the criticisms that Trump is operating outside the boundaries of law and common decency.

“We are watching the relentless escalation of violence by a lawless authoritarian regime,” said Rep. Jim Himes (D-Conn.). 

Notably, Trump has even seemed to see the writing on the wall and has attempted to turn the ship around to some degree. He told The Wall Street Journal that he was not sure if the agent who shot and killed Pretti acted appropriately — walking back comments made by Noem, among others.

Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche furthered that effort on Monday, saying in an interview that Pretti’s actions do not amount to domestic terrorism, as Noem and Stephen Miller had initially charged. 

Trump’s decision to install Tom Homan atop the administration’s efforts in Minneapolis also received plaudits from lawmakers who believe that will be of help. 

“This is a positive development — one that I hope leads to turning down the temperature and restoring order in Minnesota,” Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) wrote on social media. 

The Minneapolis shooting is also threatening to blow up Congress’s delicate effort to pass a final bloc of government spending bills before a Jan. 30 shutdown deadline. The House has passed all of its funding bills, and the Senate is set to hold its first vote on the final six-bill “minibus,” which funds scores of government departments, including Defense, Health and Human Services, Transportation, Labor and Housing and Urban Development. 

The package would also fund DHS, which Democrats are now vowing to oppose en masse unless new provisions are added to restrict the conduct of ICE and other federal immigration officers. Specifically, Democrats are calling for Republicans to strip out the DHS portion and pass the other five bills separately — a move that is difficult for the GOP.

Republicans remain supportive of funding DHS for other reasons, including to boost the Coast Guard, FEMA and TSA. Senate Appropriations Committee Chair Susan Collins (R-Maine) made a pitch on the floor on Monday, noting that 80 percent of the DHS portion goes toward non-immigration or border security items, adding that a possible shutdown would be “harmful, unnecessary and disastrous.”

Similarly, GOP leaders need unanimous consent to strip the DHS funding bill from the full appropriations package — something that is highly unlikely to happen. 

This has pushed this latest funding effort to the brink of a shutdown. The Senate will reconvene on Tuesday, having nixed its Monday session due to the snowstorm that affected much of the country. The first vote on the minibus is slated for Thursday. 

“Republicans and the White House have reached out but have not yet raised any realistic solutions,” a Senate Democratic leadership aide said.

Even Sen. John Fetterman (Pa.), a Democrat who frequently sides with Trump, said Pretti’s killing is evidence that Congress needs to force new guardrails on ICE, though he cautioned he “will never vote to shut our government down, especially our Defense Department.”

“I reject the calls to defund or abolish ICE,” Fetterman said Monday in a statement. [But] I strongly disagree with many strategies and practices ICE deployed in Minneapolis, and believe that must change.”

data.title: Former NBA player Chris Dudley announces bid to unseat Oregon's governor
data.description:
Former NBA player Chris Dudley on Monday announced his bid to unseat incumbent Oregon Gov. Tina Kotek (D) by vying for the Republican nomination. 

Marion County Commissioner Danielle Bethell is also competing in the GOP primary. 

Dudley launched a gubernatorial campaign in 2010, after playing 16 seasons of professional basketball for the Cleveland Cavaliers, New Jersey Nets, Portland Trail Blazers, New York Knicks and Phoenix Suns.

“I love Oregon, and even though we have some serious problems, there are solutions, and I believe our best days are ahead of us,” he wrote in a Facebook post announcing his second campaign.

“However, in order to solve these challenges, the empty promises, the name calling, finger pointing and fear mongering that has solved nothing must stop. You deserve better,” he added, asking for Oregonians to back his leadership bid.

He said his administration would focus on quality education, safety, affordability and good jobs.

Dudley’s decision to run for office a second time, follows an uptick in immigration enforcement in Portland, Ore., where two people were shot earlier this month.

data.title: Coast Guard suspends search for lone drug boat strike survivor in Pacific
data.description:
The U.S. Coast Guard suspended the search on Sunday evening for the lone survivor of the U.S. military’s strike on an alleged drug boat in the eastern Pacific on Friday, an operation during which two suspected “narco-terrorists” were killed on impact. 

The Coast Guard ended its effort at 7:46 p.m. PST after 56 hours of searching, during which personnel covered a total of 1,055 nautical miles with “no signs of survivors or debris,” Lt. Cmdr. Lauren Giancola, the spokesperson for the U.S. Coast Guard Southwest District, told The Hill in a statement on Monday. 

Giancola said that the Coast Guard’s HC-130J Super Hercules aircraft from the Air Station Barbers Point in Kalaeloa, Hawaii, which was “conducting missions” in support of the Southwest District at the time of the attack, was directed to the “scene to assist” in the search for the individual. 

Officials said U.S. forces notified the Coast Guard to activate the search and rescue operation shortly after carrying out the strike and identifying a survivor.

The U.S. Southern Command (Southcom) said the vessel was operated by a designated terrorist organization, transiting along “known narco-trafficking routes” and conducting “narco-trafficking operations.” It did not name the suspected terrorist group. 

The Jan. 23 attack marked the first publicly disclosed drug boat strike against an alleged drug-smuggling vessel in the Southcom region since U.S. Special Forces captured Nicolás Maduro. 

Similarly, in early January, the Coast Guard ended its search for survivors in the water after the U.S. military struck a convoy of alleged drug-smuggling vessels in the eastern Pacific. 

Since Sept. 2, the U.S. military has conducted a minimum of 36 boat strikes and killed at least 125 “narco-terrorists,” in what U.S. officials argue is a campaign to stem the flow of illegal drugs in the region and protect the U.S. The strikes have taken place in both the Caribbean Sea and the Pacific Ocean.

data.title: Education Department, HHS seeks further action against Minnesota over transgender athletes policy
data.description:
The Education Department and the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) announced on Monday they are seeking enforcement action against Minnesota over its policies around transgender athletes. 

The two federal agencies opened a Title IX investigation against the state’s Department of Education and High School League due to policies allowing transgender girls on women’s sports teams.

The agencies found the state guilty of a Title IX violation back in September and offered an agreement to resolve the violation, but the state refused it in December.

“Despite repeated opportunities to comply with Title IX, Minnesota has chosen defiance – continuing to jeopardize the safety of women and girls, deny them fair competition, and erode their right to equal access in educational programs and activities,” said Secretary of Education Linda McMahon. “As Minnesota reels from a massive fraud scandal exposing Governor Tim Walz’s dereliction of duty, today’s referral to DOJ underscores the state’s ongoing failure to safeguard its citizens and uphold the rule of law. The Trump Administration will not stop until accountability is delivered for Minnesota’s students.”

The Justice Department will continue the proceedings, with a potential loss of funding for the state from the Education Department and HHS at stake.

The Hill has reached out to Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz’s (D) office for comment.  

The referral comes as the Trump administration has been battling with Minnesota for weeks over Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) in Minneapolis and allegations of fraud at day care centers.

After weeks of protests and the deaths of two civilians at the hands of federal law enforcement, some border patrol agents are leaving Minnesota. The latest shooting rattled even some Republican lawmakers, who are calling for a transparent investigation into the matter. 

The immigration crackdown chaos happened around the same time as allegations that day care centers in Minnesota were taking federal money but had no children at their facilities.

data.title: GOP senator hits Noem's 'premature' Minneapolis shooting response
data.description:
Sen. John Curtis (R-Utah) on Monday criticized Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem’s reaction to the killing of Alex Pretti by a federal agent as “premature” and argued that she “weakened confidence” among the public as the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown comes under fire.

“We must have a transparent, independent investigation into the Minnesota shooting, and those responsible—no matter their title—must be held accountable,” Curtis, a first-term senator, wrote on the social platform X. “Officials who rush to judgment before all the facts are known undermine public trust and the law-enforcement mission.” 

“I disagree with Secretary Noem’s premature DHS response, which came before all the facts were known and weakened confidence,” he continued. “I will be working with a bipartisan group of senators to demand real oversight and transparency, including supporting calls from @RandPaul for leaders of these operations to testify, so trust can be restored and justice served.”

The statement notably adds a GOP voice to the growing criticsm of Noem, who Democrats have panned since she likened Pretti to a “domestic terrorist” in the immediate aftermath of Saturday’s shooting.

“[Pretti] had a weapon on him, and multiple – dozens – of rounds of ammunition; wishing to inflict harm on these officers, coming, brandishing like that,” she said about the 37-year-old ICU nurse at a Veterans’ Affairs hospital.

“Violence against a government because of ideological reasons and for reasons to resist and to perpetuate violence. That is the definition of domestic terrorism,” she added.

There is no evidence Pretti brandished a gun, for which he had a valid concealed carry permit.

Democratic calls to oust Noem have also spiked, with the list of cosponsors on articles of impeachment against her growing to 140. Sen. Jacky Rosen (D-Nev.) also indicated that she supports that push, with others calling on her to resign.
